updates.summary: Elon Musk's xAI built 'Colossus,' the world's largest AI supercomputer, in Boxtown — a 90% Black neighborhood in southwest Memphis first settled by formerly enslaved people in 1863. The facility operates 33 methane-powered gas turbines (permitted for only 15) without appropriate Clean Air Act permits, increasing Memphis's smog-forming NOx emissions by 30-60%. University of Tennessee researchers found peak nitrogen dioxide levels increased 79% near the facility. Over 2,000 public comments were submitted to the Shelby County Health Department opposing the turbines. The SELC filed a 60-day notice of intent to sue on behalf of the NAACP for Clean Air Act violations. Despite protests, the Shelby County Health Department approved xAI's air quality permit. Community groups — including Memphis Community Against Pollution, Protect Our Aquifer, and Young Gifted & Green — formed a coalition opposing the project. State Rep. Justin J. Pearson has been a vocal opponent. xAI leased 552 acres in Frank C. Pidgeon Industrial Park and plans to expand to 300MW+ consuming 5.7 million gallons of water per day. The facility's MLGW electricity bills averaged $4.5 million/month in 2025.

xAI purchased a power plant in Southaven and plans to invest $20B+ in data centers in DeSoto County (Colossus 2). The company installed 27 unpermitted gas turbines capable of generating 495MW — equivalent to a conventional power plant — without obtaining any permits. The turbines roar 'like jet engines' day and night, causing respiratory problems and insomnia for residents. The Safe and Sound Coalition gathered 900+ signatures demanding shutdown. At a Feb 2026 public hearing packed with hundreds of attendees, not a single person spoke in favor of xAI's proposed permanent air permit. Earthjustice and the SELC, on behalf of the NAACP, sent a 60-day notice of intent to sue for Clean Air Act violations. The facility would likely be the largest industrial source of NOx in the 11-county Memphis metropolitan area. Governor Tate Reeves called it the largest private investment in Mississippi history.

Roundhouse Digital Infrastructure planned a $100M, 10MW data center at the Port of Cascade Locks in the Columbia River Gorge, about 40 miles east of Portland. The facility would have more than doubled the area's power draw. Residents raised concerns about electricity supply impacts, the developer's lack of experience, and unclear funding. Opposition was a key issue in local elections that saw voters recall two officials who backed the project, with two other data center advocates losing re-election bids. Following the recall, the new port authority board canceled the project in July 2023. Friends of the Columbia Gorge was a key opposition group.

DeKalb County approved a 100-day moratorium on data center applications and permits in July 2024, later extended multiple times — to October 2025, then December 2025, and finally to June 2026. Commissioner Edward 'Ted' Terry warned that residential power bills could rise by an average of $20/month if data centers expand unchecked. Residents raised concerns about noise, pollution, environmental impacts, and equity, describing data centers as 'unsustainable and harmful to health.' The county currently has no restrictions, meaning data centers could be built near Doraville, Lithonia, and school campuses. The county is developing proposed regulations to address data center siting and impacts.

North Tonawanda Common Council unanimously approved a two-year moratorium on all crypto mining and data center developments in July 2024. The ban covers new crypto mining plants, expansion of existing ones, and broadly includes AI processing, general data computing, and other large-scale electronic facilities. The goal is to update the city's zoning codes to address high noise levels from existing crypto mining operations. A separate court victory rescinded approval of a fracked gas power plant sale to a cryptocurrency mining company (Digihost) in the city. Digihost agreed to reimburse up to $30,000 for the city's zoning update costs.

Provident Realty Advisors proposed a $1.3B data center on the 200-acre former Brassie Golf Course site along Pearson Road. The plan called for eight buildings at 150,000 sq ft each with backup generators. At a Memorial Day meeting, 60-75 residents gathered at Dogwood Park voicing concerns about noise, air quality, water, wetlands, wildlife, property values, and mental health impacts. The developer proposed landscaping buffers and noise suppressors. The Town Council declared it could 'never support this project' at its current scale, and Provident abandoned the proposal on June 10, 2024. The developer subsequently moved to Burns Harbor with a similar proposal.

On September 3, 2024, the Atlanta City Council voted to ban data centers within a half-mile of MARTA rail stops and along the 22-mile Beltline trail loop. Councilmembers Jason Dozier and Matt Westmoreland sponsored the legislation to preserve mixed-use development, housing, trails, and green space in prime neighborhoods like Downtown and Grove Park. Mayor Andre Dickens supported the ban, stating data centers limit jobs, strain utilities, and negatively impact communities. Existing data centers operated by QTS and DataBank in the Beltline Overlay district are grandfathered. The council later rejected a separate data center proposal in December 2024.

Fairfax County Board of Supervisors voted 8-2 on September 10, 2024 to adopt a comprehensive Data Center Zoning Ordinance Amendment. Key provisions: data centers must be built at least 200 feet from residential areas (500 feet for ground-mounted equipment like generators); minimum 1-mile distance from Metro stations; all cooling/ventilating equipment must be fully enclosed; mandatory noise studies before site plan approval; new architectural requirements for building design; 80,000 sq ft size limits in some districts; Special Exception permits required for most new data centers. Site plans accepted before July 16, 2024 are grandfathered under prior rules.

Peculiar originally added a 'data center' definition to its light industrial zoning, enabling Diode Ventures' proposed $1.5B Harper Road Technology Park — a 500-acre campus with multiple buildings next to homes and businesses. A Facebook group called 'Don't Dump Data in Peculiar' grew to nearly 1,000 members (about 1/6 of the city's population). After hundreds of residents called on local leaders to reverse course, the Planning Commission Board announced on October 3, 2024 an ordinance removing data centers from the zoning code, effectively blocking the project. The mayor claimed aldermen were pressured by a 'boisterous and ill-informed group.'

Gilmer County in the North Georgia mountains became the only Georgia county to enact a full ban on cryptocurrency mining. The ban followed organized opposition led by Cyndie Roberson, cofounder of 'Gilmer County Citizens Against Crypto Mining,' who brought hundreds of residents to a planning commission meeting. A Change.org petition ('Stop Gilmer County from allowing a Crypto Mining facility in our county') gathered signatures. Opponents cited extreme noise, massive electricity and water drain, and insufficient job creation. The county unanimously passed crypto and land use ordinances on first reading.
opposition_groups: Gilmer County Citizens Against Crypto Mining

updates.summary: Thorndale passed a formal resolution opposing a proposed 15MW bitcoin mining facility by Data Factory Austin on County Road 437 in Milam County. Dozens of residents expressed concerns about noise, water supply, and energy consumption (15MW equals about 10,500 homes' daily electricity). Residents referenced noise complaints from Data Factory's existing 10MW mine near Rockdale, where noise peaked at 99 decibels — louder than a subway. The city signed the resolution to show solidarity with citizens and sent it to local and state leaders to prevent construction.

Over 200 people packed the Betty Hagel Memorial Civic Center in Center, ND as Oliver County leaders debated a proposed moratorium on data centers and cryptocurrency mining. Residents voiced concerns about noise, heat, space, and rising utility rates, demanding to know who is building data centers and their long-term plans. The zoning board voted 4-3 to recommend a 3-year moratorium, which was formally adopted by the County Commission on December 12, 2024 covering both solar projects and data center facilities. Minnkota Power submitted a letter opposing the moratorium.

Provident Realty Advisors — after being rejected in Chesterton — proposed a seven-building, 102-acre data center campus at the corner of S. Babcock and Haglund Roads on the Worthington Steel property. Buildings could reach 120 feet tall. Over 100 people attended the August 5 Planning Commission meeting, with 20 speaking against the proposal citing unclear community benefits, fire risks, noise, and impact on the nearby Indiana Dunes National Park. The Plan Commission postponed its vote amid public outcry. As of February 2025, Provident announced intentions to revise its proposal and conduct further community outreach, with no formal application advanced.

Fundamental Data, a Virginia-based company, applied for an air permit for the 'Ridgeline Facility' — a natural gas power plant and data center campus between Thomas and Davis that could span 10,000 acres across Tucker and Grant counties. The permit application was heavily redacted, preventing residents from quantifying environmental impacts. Tucker United formed in March 2025 and mobilized hundreds of opponents. Over 2,000 people signed a petition to Governor Morrissey asking him to veto HB 2014, a law stripping local control over 'high-impact' data centers. Tucker United, the West Virginia Highlands Conservancy, and the Sierra Club appealed the DEP air permit, but the Air Quality Board sided with the developers in February 2026. Protesters traveled to Charleston to demand lawmakers restore local authority. HB 2014 diverts property tax revenue away from local bodies.

During a rare winter storm on January 22, 2025, Marion County Council approved a $2.4B data center under the code name 'Project Liberty' while most residents were snowed in and unaware. The project is backed by Stream Data Centers, operating through a legal entity called Eagle Myra, LLC. Key personnel include Anthony Bolner (founding partner) and Osin O Murchu (chief development officer). Council members had signed NDAs barring public disclosure. At the meeting, community members pressed council members on how little the public knew. Joel Rogers, a council member, spoke against the project. The case became a national symbol of how developers use NDAs and secrecy to push projects through communities — often majority-Black rural counties — with less organized political power. Marion County is predominantly Black.
updates.company: Stream Data Centers (via Eagle Myra LLC)

updates.summary: Logistics Land Investment proposed Project Marvel, a $14.5B, 4.5 million sq ft data center requiring clearing 700 acres of forest in Bessemer — a city that is approximately 70% Black with a legacy of pollution from steel mills, coal plants, and coke ovens. The NAACP (Alabama State Conference, Metro Birmingham Branch, and Center for Environmental and Climate Justice) formally opposed the project, demanding transparency, disclosure of NDAs, expected employment and tax impacts, energy/water usage details, and guarantees against utility cost increases. The project would require 2 million gallons of water daily. Despite stiff opposition and 7,000+ petition signatures, Bessemer changed its laws to accommodate data centers in October 2025, passing the project forward.
opposition_groups: NAACP Center for Environmental and Climate Justice

updates.summary: Maryland-based developer Natelli Investments LLC filed plans for the 'New Hill Digital Campus,' a 300MW data center complex on 190 acres off Old U.S. Highway 1 near Apex, requiring annexation and rezoning. The facility would consume up to 1 million gallons of reclaimed water per day during peak summer heat. Over 4,500 people signed a Change.org petition opposing the project. Residents formed the Protect Wake County Coalition to coordinate opposition. Concerns include noise, air pollution from backup generators, water use, strain on the power grid, loss of farmland, and impact on nearby schools. The neighboring Chatham County approved a 12-month moratorium on data centers and crypto mining in response to the regional proposal wave.

Denver-based Tract proposed the $30B Mooresville Technology Park on 400 acres of undeveloped land owned by Teresa Earnhardt, widow of NASCAR champion Dale Earnhardt. The project drew intense community backlash. Dale Earnhardt's eldest son Kerry and his wife Rene posted a YouTube video detailing their opposition, saying their father would be 'furious' to see his name associated with the project. About 200 people crowded a Board of Commissioners meeting wearing red T-shirts to signal opposition, with all 10 speakers raising concerns about water use in a drought-prone region and skepticism about job promises. Tract withdrew the rezoning request on August 13, 2025.

Developers proposed a data center campus offering up to 1.5GW of capacity on 209 acres in Blakely Borough — requiring power equivalent to the entire output of the nearby Lackawanna Energy Center gas-fired power plant. The first phase called for four two-story buildings at 150,000 sq ft per floor. About 100 residents packed the Borough Building with 60 more outside and 210+ watching via Zoom for an August 13, 2025 Q&A session. A grassroots petition gathered roughly 800 signatures opposing the project. The developers withdrew their zoning application on September 12, 2025, days before a scheduled public hearing. The borough subsequently adopted an ordinance applying nearly three dozen restrictions on future data center developments.

Hampden Township Commissioners unanimously rejected a proposed zoning ordinance amendment that would have designated office-park zones for data centers. Some 300 residents signed a petition, and commissioners heard more than two hours of public comment from dozens of people, most opposing the change. Food & Water Watch's eastern Pennsylvania organizer Virginia Marcille-Kerslake mobilized opposition through a door-to-door campaign with flyers and QR codes. Residents raised concerns about air, light, and noise impacts. The rejection was part of a broader wave of Pennsylvania communities proactively restricting data centers.

Applied Digital's $7B Polaris Forge 1 campus in Ellendale hosts a 250MW data center leased to CoreWeave for 15 years. Construction is underway despite community resistance. At a packed city meeting, residents wearing 'No Eminent Domain' t-shirts and carrying 'My Land, My Choice' signs confronted elected officials about lack of representation. Renee Hutchinson, who retired to Ellendale before the facility was announced, cited grid stability concerns. Leon Westerhausen opposed the project, warning it would affect future land development. The facility requested annexation into the city of Ellendale in late 2024. North Dakota lawmakers are now considering legislation to address rifts between infrastructure developers and landowners.
